title: A simulation framework for sterling money market funds: estimating redemption capacity and evaluating liquidity requirements
summary: This Bank of England Staff Working Paper (No. 1,177) by Rishabh Kumar presents a simulation framework for sterling money market funds to model redemption capacity and failure probability under various outflow timing patterns and market-liquidity conditions. The research finds that front-loaded redemptions are most destabilizing to funds and that the 30% weekly liquid asset (WLA) regulatory threshold creates cliff-edge behavior among fund managers. Simulations suggest removing this threshold effect yields significant resilience gains, with optimal WLA levels around 40% under historically extreme shocks.
key_points: Simulation framework estimates redemption capacity and failure probability across alternative redemption profiles and market-liquidity scenarios for sterling MMFs
Front-loaded redemptions are identified as most destabilizing to money market fund resilience
The 30% WLA regulatory threshold creates cliff-edge behavior where managers must consider measures to deter further redemptions
Removing the 30% WLA threshold effect yields sizeable resilience gains by reducing cliff-edge behavior
Under historically extreme shocks without threshold effects, resilience improvements concentrate around 40% WLA, with diminishing returns beyond that level
Benefit of asset sales shrinks as market depth thins
why_it_matters: This research provides quantitative guidance on optimal liquidity buffer levels for sterling money market funds and highlights how regulatory thresholds can inadvertently amplify systemic risk by triggering coordinated redemption behavior during stress. The finding that 40% WLA outperforms the 30% minimum suggests potential regulatory calibration implications for MMF liquidity requirements. For market stability monitoring, the framework offers a tool to stress-test MMF resilience under various outflow scenarios.
market_structure_context: This paper directly addresses market structure by examining money market fund liquidity management, redemption dynamics, and regulatory threshold effects on fund behavior. It provides simulation-based evidence on how the 30% WLA minimum creates systemic vulnerabilities through coordinated manager responses, and quantifies the resilience benefits of higher liquidity buffers. This informs understanding of money market fund operational resilience as part of short-term funding market infrastructure.
important_numbers: 40% (optimal WLA level for resilience under extreme shocks)
